questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Horizontal cross-section of pyramids and cones
questions.question: A plane parallel to the base cuts a triangular pyramid at a distance \(k\) from the vertex, where the pyramid's altitude is \(h\). What is the relationship between the cross-section triangle and the base triangle?

options.A: The cross-section is congruent to the base.
options.B: The cross-section is similar to the base with ratio \(k:h\).
options.C: The cross-section is similar to the base with ratio \(k^2:h^2\).
options.D: The cross-section is a different polygon.

questions.hint: Think of parallel lines creating proportional segments.
questions.explanation: Theorem 6.1 states that every cross-section parallel to the base of a triangular pyramid is a triangle similar to the base, with linear dimensions proportional to \(k/h\). Option C (\(k^2:h^2\)) applies to area ratios, not side ratios.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Horizontal cross-section of pyramids and cones
source_trace.content_anchor: Every cross-section of a triangular pyramid between the base and the vertex is a triangular region similar to the base

explanations_tiered.definition: Similar figures have the same shape but possibly different size, with corresponding sides proportional.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A horizontal cross-section of a pyramid creates a smaller similar polygon at the top.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like cutting the top off a pyramid-shaped cake — the top piece is a mini version of the whole base.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Parallel slicing preserves angles and scales all side lengths by the same factor \(k/h\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Proof uses similar triangles: \(\frac{VA'}{VA} = \frac{k}{h}\), so all corresponding sides have that ratio.
reasoning_steps: Recall that parallel lines create proportional segments.
All edges from apex to base are scaled by \(k/h\).
Therefore the cross-section polygon is similar to the base.
misconceptions: Option C (k²:h²) confuses area ratio with side ratio.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cross-section similar to base, side ratio = \(k/h\).

glossary_entry.definition: A cross-section parallel to the base of a pyramid is a polygon similar to the base.

flashcard.front: How does a horizontal cross-section of a pyramid compare to its base?
flashcard.back: It is similar, with side lengths scaled by \(k/h\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Cross-section parallel to pyramid base is ___ to base.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A cross-section parallel to the base of a pyramid is similar to the base.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use nested triangles to show proportionality.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Similar with linear ratio k:h.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Horizontal cross-section of pyramids and cones
questions.question: A triangular pyramid has a base area of \(90 \text{ cm}^2\) and an altitude of \(12\text{ cm}\). A horizontal cross-section is taken \(4\text{ cm}\) from the vertex. What is the area of this cross-section?
questions.explanation: Area ratio = \(k^2/h^2 = (4/12)^2 = (1/3)^2 = 1/9\). Cross-section area = \((1/9) \times 90 = 10 \text{ cm}^2\). Option C (30) would use \(k/h\) without squaring.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Horizontal cross-section of pyramids and cones
source_trace.content_anchor: The ratio of the area of the cross section to the area of the base is k²/h²

explanations_tiered.definition: Area of a horizontal cross-section of a pyramid scales with the square of the distance from the vertex.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If a cross-section is at fraction \(k/h\) of the altitude, its area is \((k/h)^2\) times the base area.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you cut a pyramid halfway up, the top area is one-quarter of the base, not half.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Because the cross-section is similar to the base with linear ratio \(k/h\), area ratio = \((k/h)^2\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derivation: \(A_{\text{section}} / A_{\text{base}} = (k/h)^2\) from similarity of triangles and area scaling.
worked_solution: Step 3: Multiply base area by ratio: \(90 \times 1/9 = 10\) cm².
misconceptions: Using linear ratio \(k/h\) without squaring gives 30 cm² (Option C).

glossary_entry.definition: The area of a cross-section parallel to the base is \((k/h)^2\) times the base area.

flashcard.front: How to find cross-section area in a pyramid?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Area of pyramid cross-section at 4 cm from vertex, h=12 cm, base area 90 cm²?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The area scales with the square of the distance ratio.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Cavalieri's Principle states that if two solids have equal heights and equal cross-sectional areas at every level parallel to their bases, then they have:
questions.hint: This principle relates to volume, not surface area.
questions.explanation: Cavalieri's Principle is a volume comparison principle: same height and equal cross-sectional areas at every level implies equal volumes. Option B (equal surface areas) is not guaranteed by this principle.

source_trace.content_anchor: If two solids of equal height have equal cross-sectional areas at every level parallel to the respective bases, then the two solids have equal volume

explanations_tiered.definition: Cavalieri's Principle: solids with equal heights and equal cross-sectional areas at all heights have equal volumes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This principle allows volume comparison of different-shaped solids.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like stacking cards — if each stack has the same number of cards (area per level), the total volume (height × area) is the same.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Used to derive volume formulas for pyramids, cones, and spheres without integration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Formally: If \(A_1(z) = A_2(z)\) for all \(z\), then \(\int A_1(z) dz = \int A_2(z) dz\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of Cavalieri's Principle.
misconceptions: Cavalieri does not guarantee equal surface areas (Option B).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cavalieri: equal height + equal cross-sectional areas → equal volumes.

glossary_entry.definition: Two solids with equal heights and equal cross-sectional areas at every level have equal volumes.

flashcard.back: Volumes of solids with equal heights and equal cross-sectional areas.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Cavalieri's Principle states: equal heights and equal cross-sectional areas → equal ___.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Volumes are equal if heights and all cross-sectional areas match.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with stacked coins versus tilted stack.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the name of the part of a pyramid that remains after cutting off the top by a plane parallel to the base?
questions.hint: This solid has two parallel polygonal bases.
questions.explanation: A frustum of a pyramid is the portion of a pyramid between the base and a parallel cutting plane. Option B (cross-section) is just the cut face, not the remaining solid.

source_trace.content_anchor: A frustum of a pyramid is part of the pyramid between the base and a cross-section of the pyramid

explanations_tiered.definition: A frustum of a pyramid is the solid between two parallel planes cutting a pyramid, excluding the top small pyramid.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: It has two polygonal bases — the original base and a smaller similar top.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like a pyramid with its top cut off flat, making a flat top and bottom.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The lateral faces become trapezoids instead of triangles.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The frustum's volume can be found by subtracting the small top pyramid from the original pyramid.
reasoning_steps: Visualise cutting a pyramid parallel to its base.
The lower part (between base and cut) is the frustum.
misconceptions: Cross-section (Option B) is just the cut surface, not the whole solid.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Frustum = pyramid with top cut off by a plane parallel to base.

glossary_entry.definition: The part of a pyramid between its base and a parallel cutting plane.

flashcard.front: What is a frustum of a pyramid?
flashcard.back: Pyramid with top cut off by a plane parallel to the base.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What remains after cutting top off pyramid parallel to base?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The lower part of a pyramid after cutting off the top by a plane parallel to the base.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with cross-section (just the cut face).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Frustum is the remaining part after parallel cut.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What shape are the lateral faces of a frustum of a regular pyramid?
questions.hint: Each lateral face connects a side of the top base to a side of the bottom base.
questions.explanation: In a frustum of a regular pyramid, each lateral face is an isosceles trapezoid because the top and bottom edges are parallel and the non-parallel sides (slant edges) are equal. Option A (triangles) describes the original pyramid's lateral faces.

source_trace.content_anchor: The lateral faces of a frustum of a regular pyramid are congruent isosceles trapeziums

explanations_tiered.definition: Isosceles trapezoids have one pair of parallel sides and equal non-parallel sides.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each lateral face of a frustum connects a side of the top base to a side of the bottom base.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like a flat-topped pyramid — each side is a trapezoid, not a triangle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The top edge (small base side) is parallel to the bottom edge (large base side), and the lateral edges are equal in length.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In a regular pyramid frustum, symmetry ensures that all lateral faces are congruent isosceles trapezoids.
reasoning_steps: Recall that a frustum is a pyramid with top cut off.
Each original triangular face becomes a trapezoid after cutting.
Because the cut is parallel to the base, the top and bottom edges are parallel.
misconceptions: Original pyramid has triangular faces (Option A), but frustum's lateral faces are trapezoids.

glossary_entry.definition: The trapezoidal faces connecting the two bases of a frustum.

flashcard.front: Shape of lateral faces in frustum of regular pyramid?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Lateral face shape of a frustum of a regular pyramid?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Trapezoidal faces connecting the two bases of a frustum.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Frustum lateral faces = trapezoids, not triangles.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with original pyramid's triangular faces.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Lateral faces become isosceles trapezoids after parallel cut.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A frustum of a regular square pyramid has lower base side \(5\text{ cm}\), upper base side \(3\text{ cm}\), and slant height \(4\text{ cm}\). What is its lateral surface area?
questions.explanation: Lower perimeter \(p = 4 \times 5 = 20\), upper perimeter \(p' = 4 \times 3 = 12\). LSA = \(\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times (20 + 12) = 2 \times 32 = 64 \text{ cm}^2\). Option B (48) might come from \(l(p + p')\) without the half.

explanations_tiered.definition: Lateral surface area of a frustum is the sum of areas of its trapezoidal lateral faces.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Formula uses average perimeter times slant height.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Add up the areas of the four trapezoid sides.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Each trapezoid area = \(\frac{1}{2} l (\text{top side} + \text{bottom side})\); summing gives \(\frac{1}{2} l (p + p')\).

formula_analysis.name: Lateral surface area of frustum of regular pyramid

misconceptions: Forgetting the \(\frac{1}{2}\) gives 96 cm² (not an option here) or miscalculating perimeters.

glossary_entry.definition: The total area of the trapezoidal lateral faces of a frustum.

flashcard.front: Lateral area formula for frustum of regular pyramid?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: LSA of square frustum: side5, side3, slant4?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use net of frustum to visualise trapezoids.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A right circular cone of altitude \(8\text{ cm}\) and base radius \(6\text{ cm}\) is cut to form a frustum of height \(4\text{ cm}\). What is the lateral surface area of the frustum?
questions.hint: LSA frustum = LSA(big cone) − LSA(small cone).
questions.explanation: Big cone: \(l = \sqrt{6^2+8^2}=10\), LSA = \(\pi(6)(10)=60\pi\). Small cone (top): height = \(8-4=4\), radius similar: \(r' = 3\), \(l' = \sqrt{3^2+4^2}=5\), LSA = \(\pi(3)(5)=15\pi\). Difference = \(45\pi\text{ cm}^2\). Option A (30π) would come from wrong radius.

source_trace.content_anchor: LSA of frustum = LSA of bigger cone - LSA of smaller cone

explanations_tiered.definition: Lateral surface area of a frustum equals the difference between the lateral areas of the original cone and the removed top cone.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Find radii and slant heights of both cones using similarity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The frustum's curved surface is what's left after cutting off the top cone.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Use similar triangles to find top cone's radius (3 cm) and slant height (5 cm).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Subtract small cone LSA from big cone LSA: \(\pi r l - \pi r' l'\).
worked_solution: Step 2: Ratio = 4/8 = 1/2, so top radius = \(6/2=3\) cm.
reasoning_steps: Use similarity to find top radius.

formula_analysis.name: Lateral area of cone frustum by subtraction

misconceptions: Using \(l = \sqrt{r^2 + (h_{\text{frustum}})^2}\) instead of full cone height.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Frustum LSA = big cone LSA − small cone LSA.

glossary_entry.term: Lateral area of frustum by subtraction
glossary_entry.definition: The lateral area of a frustum equals the difference between the lateral areas of the original and removed cones.

flashcard.front: How to find LSA of cone frustum?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: LSA of frustum from cone r=6,h=8, frustum height 4?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Difference of lateral areas of original and removed cones.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Frustum LSA = big LSA − small LSA.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use similarity to find top cone dimensions.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Big LSA=60π, small=15π, difference=45π cm².

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A frustum of a cone has top diameter \(6\text{ m}\), bottom diameter \(10\text{ m}\), and height \(12\text{ m}\). What is its volume? (Use \(\pi = 3.14\))
questions.hint: Volume of frustum = \(\frac{1}{3}\pi h (r^2 + r'^2 + rr')\).
questions.explanation: Radii: \(r = 5\text{ m}\), \(r' = 3\text{ m}\). \(V = \frac{1}{3}\pi(12)(25+9+15) = 4\pi(49) = 196\pi \text{ m}^3\). Numerically: \(196 \times 3.14 = 615.44\text{ m}^3\). Option D has \(\pi\) incorrectly attached.

explanations_tiered.definition: Volume of a frustum of a cone is the space between the two parallel bases.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Formula combines areas of both circular bases and their geometric mean.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find volume of original cone, subtract top cone, or use the special formula.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived by subtracting small cone volume from big cone volume using similar triangles.
misconceptions: Leaving π in final numeric answer (Option D) is incorrect when π=3.14 is given.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cone frustum volume: \(\frac{1}{3}\pi h (r^2 + r'^2 + rr')\).

glossary_entry.definition: The space inside a truncated cone.

flashcard.front: Volume formula for frustum of a cone?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Volume of frustum d=10, d'=6, h=12?

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A frustum of a pyramid has rectangular bases: lower base \(4\text{ m} \times 5\text{ m}\), upper base area \(10\text{ m}^2\), and height \(15\text{ m}\). Find its volume.
questions.explanation: Lower area \(A = 4 \times 5 = 20\text{ m}^2\), \(A' = 10\text{ m}^2\). \(V = \frac{1}{3} \times 15 \times (20 + 10 + \sqrt{20 \times 10}) = 5 \times (30 + \sqrt{200}) = 5 \times (30 + 10\sqrt{2}) = 5 \times (30 + 14.14) = 220.7\text{ m}^3\). Option A would result from omitting the √ term.

explanations_tiered.definition: Volume of a frustum of any pyramid uses the areas of both bases and their geometric mean.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Formula \(V = \frac{1}{3}h(A + A' + \sqrt{AA'})\) works for any frustum (pyramid or cone).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Average of the base areas in a special way, times height, over 3.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The \(\sqrt{AA'}\) term accounts for the middle section shape.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived by subtracting small pyramid from big pyramid using similarity and algebra.
misconceptions: Omitting \(\sqrt{AA'}\) gives \(5 \times 30 = 150\) (not an option) but something similar to A.

glossary_entry.definition: Space inside a truncated pyramid or cone.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Volume of frustum: A=20, A'=10, h=15?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate volumes of frustums of pyramids

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Why does the volume formula for a frustum include the term \(\sqrt{AA'}\)?

options.A: To average the two base areas
options.B: To account for the geometric mean of the areas
options.C: To make the formula equal to a prism volume
options.D: To correct for the slant height

questions.hint: This term comes from the product of corresponding sides in similar figures.
questions.explanation: The term \(\sqrt{AA'}\) is the geometric mean of the two base areas, arising from the square of the linear scale factor when deriving the formula from similar pyramids. Option A (average) would be \((A+A')/2\), which is not correct.

explanations_tiered.definition: Geometric mean of two numbers is the square root of their product.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In frustum volume, \(\sqrt{AA'}\) appears from the product of the linear scale factor squared.
explanations_tiered.beginner: It's the 'middle' area between the top and bottom that makes the formula work.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From similarity, if linear ratio = \(k\), then areas scale by \(k^2\), leading to the term \(\sqrt{A A'}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derivation: Let top area = \(k^2 A\), then \(\sqrt{AA'} = \sqrt{A \cdot k^2 A} = kA\), which appears in algebra when subtracting pyramid volumes.
reasoning_steps: Recall that area scales with square of linear ratio.
The geometric mean naturally appears when combining similar shapes.
misconceptions: The term is not an arithmetic mean (average), which would be \((A+A')/2\).

glossary_entry.definition: The square root of the product of two base areas, appearing in frustum volume formula.

flashcard.front: What does \(\sqrt{AA'}\) represent in frustum volume?
flashcard.back: Geometric mean of the two base areas.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is \(\sqrt{AA'}\) in frustum volume formula?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It's the geometric mean of base areas from similarity scaling.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The square root of the product of two numbers.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Derive from similar pyramid volume subtraction.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. It's the geometric mean from similarity.
